EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with very cold air aloft to produce showers and thunderstorms across much of Southern New England. Many of these storms produced large hail.
A strong upper level disturbance combined with very cold air aloft to produce showers and thunderstorms across much of Southern New England. Many of these storms produced large hail.

Showers and thunderstorms developed during the morning as a slow moving frontal boundary crossed southern New England. A tropical airmass was in place which led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding affected several streets including Narragansett Avenue, Southwest Avenue along the seawall, Shoreby Hill, and Jamestown Shores.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the morning as a slow moving frontal boundary crossed southern New England. A tropical airmass was in place which led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding affected parts of the downtown Warren area. Route 136 was flooded with at least one foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England. In addition, both downed leaves from the storm and naturally fallen leaves from before the storm clogged storm drains which resulted in street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were downed onto Hope Street or Route 114 in Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over New York City lifted northeast across Southern New England. This worked together with a warm humid airmass to produce heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads flooded and impassable.  Several cars trapped and stalled due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over New York City lifted northeast across Southern New England. This worked together with a warm humid airmass to produce heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 44 in Chepachet and Greenville Center was flooded with 2 feet of standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over New York City lifted northeast across Southern New England. This worked together with a warm humid airmass to produce heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water spilled over the seawall at Portsmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England. In addition, both downed leaves from the storm and naturally fallen leaves from before the storm clogged storm drains which resulted in street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Boombridge Road and Hardwood Lane in Westerly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England. In addition, both downed leaves from the storm and naturally fallen leaves from before the storm clogged storm drains which resulted in street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Laurel Avenue and Washington Street were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds gusting as high as 55 mph ahead of a cold front produced minor damage near Providence and the South Coast. A little more than one inch of rain in a short time resulted in significant urban flooding in parts of Cranston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over one inch of rain in a short time resulted in flooding on parts of Oaklawn Avenue, Atwood Avenue, and Plainfield Street in Cranston where as much as 1 to 2 feet of water covered the road. One car was stranded in flood waters on Wilbur Avenue. The right lane of I-95 northbound by Route 10 was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal storm brought wintry precipitation, heavy rain, and strong winds to much of southern New England. A mix of precipitation brought freezing drizzle, snow, and sleet to northwestern portions of the area while heavy rain was widespread across southeastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, as well as portions of northern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eighth of an inch of ice accumulated on exposed surfaces in the western part of Glocester.  Numerous accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying coastal low pressure system moved up the coast from North Carolina, crossing the benchmark (40 N/70 W) southeast of Nantucket. This storm brought strong to damaging winds to much of New England and produced minor coastal flooding along the east coast of Massachusetts. Little precipitation was received with this storm, with less than 1 to 2 inches of snow reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed closing a road in Middletown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving over southern New England brought heavy rain, strong to damaging winds, and a convective line of showers and thunderstorms to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator on Block Island recorded a 58 mph wind gust on their home weather station.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought six to twelve inches of snow across much of southern New England. Lesser amounts fell east of the Interstate 95 corridor where snow changed to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to ten inches of snow fell across northwestern Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving off the mid-Atlantic coast intensified as it moved northeastward over Nantucket. This spread heavy snow across all of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of snow fell across Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved off the DelMarVa peninsula and moved northeastward passing southeast of southern New England.  This brought strong winds and heavy snow to the southern portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of snow fell across eastern Kent County, including at T.F. Green State Airport in Warwick, RI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 58 year old man was swept away by high surf while fishing on rocks at Beavertail State Park in Jamestown, RI.  Tropical Storm Bertha was spinning off the coast of North Carolina, churning up seas and sending them northward, contributing to the high surf along the Rhode Island coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 year old Massachusetts man was swept away by high surf while fishing on rocks at Beavertail State Park in Jamestown, RI. His body was recovered from the surf along the rocky shoreline.  Tropical Storm Bertha was spinning off the coast of North Carolina, churning up seas and sending them northward, contributing to the high surf along the Rhode Island coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England. In addition, both downed leaves from the storm and naturally fallen leaves from before the storm clogged storm drains which resulted in street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed blocking Perry Hill Road in Coventry.  Power lines were downed onto Yale Drive.  In West Greenwich, a large tree and power lines were downed onto Fry Pond Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England. In addition, both downed leaves from the storm and naturally fallen leaves from before the storm clogged storm drains which resulted in street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto Division Road in East Greenwich.  In Warwick, a tree was down blocking Natick Avenue.  In West Warwick, a tree was downed onto Natick Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England. In addition, both downed leaves from the storm and naturally fallen leaves from before the storm clogged storm drains which resulted in street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of water stood in the intersection of Charles and Orms Streets.  A car was abandoned in the intersection.  A car was stuck in flood waters on Whipple Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system passed south and east of southern New England bringing widespread rainfall to much of the region. This system was anomalously moist with precipitable waters two to three standard deviations above normal for late March. Anywhere from two to five inches of rain fell across southern New England with the highest amounts falling along the south coast of Rhode Island and Massachusetts. This resulted in flash flooding across much of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of rain fell across portions of Washington County.  This resulted in flash flooding in Westerly.  Canal Street was flooded.  Spring Brook overflowed its banks in the White Rock section of Westerly.  Water overflowed four to five yards nearing the foundations of those houses.  The Pawcatuck River also overflowed its banks in the White Rock section of town.  White Rock Road had 2-3 inches of flowing water over the road near a soccer field entrance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system passed south and east of southern New England bringing widespread rainfall to much of the region. This system was anomalously moist with precipitable waters two to three standard deviations above normal for late March. Anywhere from two to five inches of rain fell across southern New England with the highest amounts falling along the south coast of Rhode Island and Massachusetts. This resulted in flash flooding across much of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant portion of Peacedale Village in South Kingstown was flooded and culverts were beyond full.  Low buildings were being sandbagged to protect them.  In Exeter, Route 102 north of Route 2 was flooded with over a foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought strong winds to parts of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at T.F. Green State Airport in Warwick (KPVD) recorded sustained wind speeds of 44 mph.  Winds gusted to 58 mph at a National Ocean Service site at Conimicut Point.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought strong winds to parts of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed at the intersection of Metcalf Avenue and Winona Street in North Providence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system passed south and east of southern New England bringing widespread rainfall to much of the region. This system was anomalously moist with precipitable waters two to three standard deviations above normal for late March. Anywhere from two to five inches of rain fell across southern New England with the highest amounts falling along the south coast of Rhode Island and Massachusetts. This resulted in flash flooding across much of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell across Bristol County.  This resulted in flash flooding across most of the county.  In Bristol, Franklin, Chestnut, and James Streets were flooded.  Route 114 near the Mount Hope Bridge and the Lobster Pot was closed due to flooding.  Ward Street was flooded and closed.  One house was evacuated at the end of the street. Several basements also were flooded.  In Warren, Main Street was flooded with 4 inches of water and Market Street was flooded with 6-8 inches of water.  Homestead Avenue was flooded with three feet of water.  Long Wharf Drive and Columbus Street were flooded with one foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall developed ahead of a warm front moving northward towards southern New England.  Rain was particularly heavy in Providence where it caused some minor street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding 12 inches deep at the intersection of Helme and Valley Streets.  The intersection of Delaine Street and Sonoma Court was flooded with 15 inches of water deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds gusting as high as 55 mph ahead of a cold front produced minor damage near Providence and the South Coast. A little more than one inch of rain in a short time resulted in significant urban flooding in parts of Cranston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed on Maxson Hill Road in Hopkinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds gusting as high as 55 mph ahead of a cold front produced minor damage near Providence and the South Coast. A little more than one inch of rain in a short time resulted in significant urban flooding in parts of Cranston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed in the area of Mineral Spring Avenue at Spicer Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through Southern New England, igniting showers and thunderstorms across the area. Many of these storms produced small to large size hail thanks to very cold temperatures aloft.
